title: Quiz: DFT for Solids
description: Test your understanding of DFT methods for solid state systems

questions.question: What determines the number of plane waves in a calculation?
questions.explanation: The plane wave cutoff E_cut determines which plane waves are included: those with kinetic energy ℏ²|k+G|²/2m < E_cut. Higher cutoff means more plane waves and better accuracy, but more computational cost.

questions.question: Why are pseudopotentials used in plane wave DFT?

options.text: To avoid the high plane wave cutoff needed for core electrons

questions.explanation: Core electron wavefunctions oscillate rapidly near the nucleus and would require very high E_cut (10,000+ eV) to describe accurately. Pseudopotentials replace the core electrons with a smooth effective potential, allowing much lower cutoffs.

questions.question: Which type of pseudopotential allows reconstruction of all-electron wavefunctions?
questions.explanation: PAW (Projector Augmented Wave) pseudopotentials can reconstruct the full all-electron wavefunction, including the nodal structure near the nucleus. This allows accurate core-level properties while maintaining computational efficiency.

questions.question: Which functional is commonly used to correct DFT band gap underestimation?
questions.explanation: HSE (Heyd-Scuseria-Ernzerhof) is a hybrid functional that includes a fraction of exact exchange. This helps correct the band gap underestimation of LDA/GGA, giving much better agreement with experiment for semiconductors.

questions.question: Metals require denser k-point grids than insulators for converged total energies.
questions.explanation: True. Metals have a Fermi surface that must be sampled accurately - the occupations change sharply at the Fermi level. Insulators have smooth, fully occupied/empty bands that converge with fewer k-points.

questions.question: Which properties require convergence testing in solid state DFT? (Select all)
questions.explanation: E_cut, k-points, and smearing width all affect accuracy and must be converged. The programming language of the code doesn't affect the physics - only the implementation.
